Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a single source using a direct HTTPS URL from the official PostgreSQL FTP server (`https://ftp.postgresql.org`), which is pgAdmin's legitimate upstream. The source is a Debian package, and the checksum is explicitly provided and matches the expected format for integrity verification. During the execution of `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`, only the sourcing of the PKGBUILD and downloading of the declared source occur. There is no command substitution, dynamic code execution, or remote code fetching at the top level or inside `pkgver()`. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic versioning logic is executed. The rest of the source fetching and checksum handling is standard and safe.

No code is executed during the sourcing phase beyond static variable assignments. The actual extraction and installation happen later in the `package()` function, which is not run due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. The commented `source` line poses no risk as it is inert. All values come from trusted, static, and verifiable sources. No data exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation (eval, base64 decoding, etc.), or remote execution is present.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source handling; no dynamic code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe source handling; no dynamic code[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a `.deb` package for `pgadmin4-desktop` from the official PostgreSQL FTP server (`ftp.postgresql.org`) using a correctly formatted URL that matches the package's upstream source. The file is a binary package, and its checksum is provided and matches the expected value, ensuring integrity. The `package()` function extracts the contents of `data.tar.zst`, which is a standard step when repackaging a `.deb` file for Arch Linux — this is safe as long as the source is trusted, which in this case it is.

There are no suspicious commands such as `curl|bash`, `eval`, or obfuscated payloads. No runtime downloads, network exfiltration attempts, or unauthorized file modifications are present. The dependencies and conflicts are appropriate and minimal. The use of `tar` with `--zstd` is valid for extracting modern `.deb` archives, which often use zstd compression. No post-install hooks or scripts modify system state beyond installation.

The commented `source` line appears to be an alternative version of the same upstream URL with a slightly different naming scheme, possibly for a different Debian release, but it is inactive and thus irrelevant. Overall, this PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a `-bin` package and shows no signs of supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Trusted source, valid checksum, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard Arch packaging file for `pgadmin4-desktop-bin`. The `source` array downloads a `.deb` file from the official PostgreSQL FTP server over HTTPS, with a pinned SHA-256 sum. There is no executable code in the global scope or in any `pkgver()` function; only variable definitions are present. The `package()` function, which extracts the archive, is not executed during the narrow gate (`--nobuild`, `--nodeps`, `--noprepare`). No dangerous commands (e.g., `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, base64 decoding) appear anywhere in the file. Fetching the source via `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` is safe.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD; no dangerous actions in scope</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard PKGBUILD; no dangerous actions in scope[/]

Audit Skips:

/tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-oaogkjof/pgadmin4-desktop-9.16-x86_64.deb: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: pgadmin4-desktop-9.16-x86_64.deb

data.reasoning: The package is a standard AUR -bin package that downloads a prebuilt .deb from the official PostgreSQL FTP server (ftp.postgresql.org) over HTTPS with a pinned SHA-256 checksum. The PKGBUILD's package() function only extracts data.tar.zst from the .deb into the package directory using standard tar — no network access, no eval, no obfuscation, no .install hooks. Both models reviewed the only meaningful executable file (PKGBUILD) and reached the same conclusion. The .deb binary cannot be statically reviewed by an LLM and is appropriately skipped; its integrity is guaranteed by the pinned checksum against a trusted upstream.
coverage_issues: The .deb binary was skipped by both models (unavoidable for LLM review), so the actual installed payload is not inspected. Integrity rests entirely on the checksum + upstream trust, which is acceptable for a -bin package but means the audit cannot certify the binary contents themselves.
Neither model explicitly confirmed the absence of .install hooks in the package (e.g., post_install) — though the PKGBUILD review implies none exist.
learnings: For -bin packages with only a PKGBUILD + binary artifact, reviewing 1 file (PKGBUILD) is actually complete coverage; the 'shallow (1 file reviewed)' trigger is a false alarm here since the only non-binary file IS the PKGBUILD. The tool should distinguish 'shallow' (skipped reviewable files) from 'minimal package' (no other reviewable files exist).
Both models gave consistent, well-reasoned verdicts focusing correctly on what executes during makepkg/package() — good signal that the prompt guidance on AUR-specific risks is working.

The tool should record whether any .install scripts or hooks are declared in the PKGBUILD as an explicit checklist item, so reviewers can confirm none were silently missed rather than inferring absence.

Consider extracting and listing the .deb's control scripts (preinst/postinst/postrm) as a future enhancement, since those can execute on the user's system at install time even though they are not run during makepkg — this is the only residual unreviewed risk surface for -bin packages.
